Also "Always use read_file to inspect the target config file BEFORE modifying it." So we can call read_file first to read content, but we can't chain commands in a single invocation. So we will first call read_file /etc/fstab, output is the content. Then we will decide modifications. But the plan will describe that.

Given that we need to use sed -i to modify file, we can.

Also we need to manage ability to add nodev only if not already present. For lines containing /boot, we need to ensure nodev exists in fourth field.

We also need to remount using mount -o remount /boot.

We should recall to always back up original file before modifications.

Make plan: "We will read /etc/fstab, back it up to /etc/fstab.bak, then use sed to modify line containing /boot, add nodev if missing, then remount /boot." We'll propose to use sed command: `sed -i '/\s\/boot\s+/s/\(\s\+\)\([a-zA-Z0-9,]*\)$/\1\2,nodev/' /etc/fstab`. But we also need to handle case with empty fourth column: domain. So we might use simpler pattern: `sed -i '/\s\/boot\s/ s/$/ nodev/' /etc/fstab` but this will add nodev only if fourth column is empty. But if fourth column had other options, we need to add it. Perhaps easier is to use regex to modify the fourth column: `sed -i '/\s\/boot\s/ s/\(\s\+\)[^#]\{0,\}\(\s\+\)/\1nodev\2/'`? This is complicated.

Alternate approach: read the file content and parse lines; build new content in memory, then write_file. That's easier and more controlled. We'll use read_file to read /etc/fstab, process each line: if it's /boot and has nodev, skip; else if it's /boot but missing nodev, modify. Then save new content. That meets "modify existing values in-place" maybe not exactly but it's okay. "Prefer minimal, reversible changes" writing new content with modifications is okay. It will avoid duplicate.

Let's do: read_file /etc/fstab. Then in code do modifications, maybe print new content. Then write_file new content. Also backup the original file.

Now plan details.
